ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper offers notable novelty by fully automating a previously semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) with a repetition penalty and by producing a publicly available, manually annotated corpus of authentic lyrics for 13 rap artists — both concrete contributions beyond prior work. Its comprehensive manual evaluation protocol tailored to rap ghostwriting (fluency, coherence, style matching) further provides a new, domain-specific methodology. The modeling choices (artist-specific LSTM vs n-gram baselines) are standard, so the technical modeling is incremental, but the evaluation tooling and dataset meaningfully differentiate the work from earlier studies. | B(3): The paper provides moderate novelty by formalizing a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol for rap ghostwriting and by fully automating a previously semi-automatic repetition-penalizing metric (Potash et al., 2015). The release of a manually annotated corpus of authentic lyrics for 13 rap artists is a concrete, new dataset contribution. However, the modeling work (artist-specific LSTM vs n-gram baselines) and preprocessing are standard techniques, making the overall contribution incremental rather than highly original.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers notable novelty by fully automating a previously semi-automatic evaluation (Potash et al., 2015) with a repetition penalty and by producing a publicly available, manually annotated corpus of authentic lyrics for 13 rap artists — both concrete contributions beyond prior work. Its comprehensive manual evaluation protocol tailored to rap ghostwriting (fluency, coherence, style matching) further provides a new, domain-specific methodology. The modeling choices (artist-specific LSTM vs n-gram baselines) are standard, so the technical modeling is incremental, but the evaluation tooling and dataset meaningfully differentiate the work from earlier studies.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides moderate novelty by formalizing a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol for rap ghostwriting and by fully automating a previously semi-automatic repetition-penalizing metric (Potash et al., 2015). The release of a manually annotated corpus of authentic lyrics for 13 rap artists is a concrete, new dataset contribution. However, the modeling work (artist-specific LSTM vs n-gram baselines) and preprocessing are standard techniques, making the overall contribution incremental rather than highly original.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear methodology and experiments (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (evaluation methodology, automation of prior method, annotated corpus). Notation and methodological details (e.g., hyperparameters reported, evaluation metrics defined) appear consistent and well explained, and examples/metrics (rhyme density, similarity scores, Match%) make the evaluation concrete. Writing quality is adequate with related work coverage appropriate, though some presentation details (e.g., the heuristic preprocessing and remaining non-verse text) could be described more precisely to avoid minor ambiguity. | B(4): The paper is well organized and reads with a logical flow (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion): the summary lists clear methodology, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics. The main contributions are stated early (manual evaluation methodology, an automated repetition penalty, and a new annotated corpus). Notation and experimental details appear sufficiently defined for reproduction (e.g., hyperparameters reported and concrete metrics like rhyme density and Match%), and figures/tables/examples are implied to be effective given the detailed reporting. Writing quality is described as "adequate," and minor presentation issues remain (e.g., heuristic preprocessing and possible dataset ambiguities) that prevent an exceptional rating.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its LSTM model against an n-gram baseline (n∈{1..9}) using the same OHHLA artist-specific corpus and shared evaluation metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, and manual annotations), and it also automates and improves a prior semi-automatic method (Potash et al., 2015). The authors acknowledge limitations of the LSTM (difficulty integrating new vocabulary) and report related work coverage as adequate, indicating fair and analyzed comparisons. Minor omissions of additional contemporary neural baselines are not indicated in the summary, so comparisons are judged good with only minor gaps. | B(4): The paper compares its LSTM ghostwriter against a range of n-gram baselines (n∈{1..9}) and directly improves/automates the prior Potash et al. (2015) semi-automatic evaluation, using the same OHHLA artist corpus and shared metrics (rhyme density, max-similarity, manual annotations). Comparisons appear fair (same data and evaluation protocol, hyperparameters reported) and prior work is acknowledged and integrated into the evaluation. Minor omissions remain — notably broader/ newer neural baselines (e.g., transformer-based models) are not included — so the comparison is good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its LSTM model against an n-gram baseline (n∈{1..9}) using the same OHHLA artist-specific corpus and shared evaluation metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity, and manual annotations), and it also automates and improves a prior semi-automatic method (Potash et al., 2015). The authors acknowledge limitations of the LSTM (difficulty integrating new vocabulary) and report related work coverage as adequate, indicating fair and analyzed comparisons. Minor omissions of additional contemporary neural baselines are not indicated in the summary, so comparisons are judged good with only minor gaps.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper addresses a meaningful problem in creative language generation by proposing a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol for rap ghostwriting and fully automating a prior semi-automatic repetition-penalty metric, which are practical contributions to evaluation practice. The release of a 13-artist corpus with manual style-similarity annotations is a valuable resource that will likely be reused by researchers, and the empirical finding that LSTM models generalize better than n-gram baselines is informative for modeling choices. However, the work is fairly specialized to rap/style matching and retains important limitations (automated evaluation cannot capture vocabulary/tone/themes; LSTM difficulty integrating new vocabulary), so its impact is moderate rather than field-changing. | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem in creative language generation by providing a comprehensive manual evaluation methodology (fluency, coherence, style matching) and fully automating a prior repetition-penalty procedure, which can materially improve and standardize evaluation practices. The public corpus of authentic lyrics for 13 artists with manual style annotations is a valuable resource that others can build on, and the empirical finding that LSTM ghostwriters generalize better than n-gram baselines offers useful guidance for model selection. While largely focused on the niche of rap-lyric ghostwriting, the evaluation techniques and automated metrics are likely to influence broader creative-text generation research and open follow-on work on style modeling and evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a meaningful problem in creative language generation by proposing a comprehensive manual evaluation protocol for rap ghostwriting and fully automating a prior semi-automatic repetition-penalty metric, which are practical contributions to evaluation practice. The release of a 13-artist corpus with manual style-similarity annotations is a valuable resource that will likely be reused by researchers, and the empirical finding that LSTM models generalize better than n-gram baselines is informative for modeling choices. However, the work is fairly specialized to rap/style matching and retains important limitations (automated evaluation cannot capture vocabulary/tone/themes; LSTM difficulty integrating new vocabulary), so its impact is moderate rather than field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem in creative language generation by providing a comprehensive manual evaluation methodology (fluency, coherence, style matching) and fully automating a prior repetition-penalty procedure, which can materially improve and standardize evaluation practices. The public corpus of authentic lyrics for 13 artists with manual style annotations is a valuable resource that others can build on, and the empirical finding that LSTM ghostwriters generalize better than n-gram baselines offers useful guidance for model selection. While largely focused on the niche of rap-lyric ghostwriting, the evaluation techniques and automated metrics are likely to influence broader creative-text generation research and open follow-on work on style modeling and evaluation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents comprehensive experiments: multi-faceted manual evaluations (line-level fluency/coherence and style-matching), automated metrics (rhyme density and maximum-similarity regression), and comparisons of an LSTM ghostwriter to n-gram baselines across n=1..9, and it also creates a manually annotated corpus for 13 artists. It includes an improved fully automated repetition penalty and reports hyperparameters, discusses failure modes (LSTM vocabulary limits, preprocessing heuristics, potential non-verse text) and gives adequate related-work coverage. However, reproducibility is only partially supported (code availability not mentioned, preprocessing remains heuristic), and there is no explicit ablation of LSTM variants or broader sensitivity analyses of the neural model, so the work is strong but has minor gaps. | B(4): The experiments are substantial: artist-specific LSTM models are evaluated against n-gram baselines across 13 artists using complementary manual annotations (line-level fluency/coherence, style-matching) and automated metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity) and an improved automatic repetition penalty. However, the paper does not report explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses of key components (e.g., the regression-based repetition penalty or model variants), and code is not mentioned even though hyperparameters are reported, which could limit exact reproducibility given heuristic preprocessing and possible dataset noise. Related work is described as adequate, and several failure modes/limitations (vocabulary integration, limits of automated evaluation) are candidly discussed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents comprehensive experiments: multi-faceted manual evaluations (line-level fluency/coherence and style-matching), automated metrics (rhyme density and maximum-similarity regression), and comparisons of an LSTM ghostwriter to n-gram baselines across n=1..9, and it also creates a manually annotated corpus for 13 artists. It includes an improved fully automated repetition penalty and reports hyperparameters, discusses failure modes (LSTM vocabulary limits, preprocessing heuristics, potential non-verse text) and gives adequate related-work coverage. However, reproducibility is only partially supported (code availability not mentioned, preprocessing remains heuristic), and there is no explicit ablation of LSTM variants or broader sensitivity analyses of the neural model, so the work is strong but has minor gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are substantial: artist-specific LSTM models are evaluated against n-gram baselines across 13 artists using complementary manual annotations (line-level fluency/coherence, style-matching) and automated metrics (rhyme density, maximum similarity) and an improved automatic repetition penalty. However, the paper does not report explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses of key components (e.g., the regression-based repetition penalty or model variants), and code is not mentioned even though hyperparameters are reported, which could limit exact reproducibility given heuristic preprocessing and possible dataset noise. Related work is described as adequate, and several failure modes/limitations (vocabulary integration, limits of automated evaluation) are candidly discussed.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The summary indicates the paper's main claims are empirically supported: artist-specific LSTM models were compared to a strong n-gram (1–9) baseline using complementary manual annotations (line-level fluency/coherence, style-matching) and automated metrics (rhyme density, max similarity regression), and known limitations are acknowledged (vocabulary integration, preprocessing noise). There are no mathematical proofs to evaluate and hyperparameters are reported, but the summary does not mention statistical tests or code release, and the dataset preprocessing may leave non-verse text—issues that are minor but leave some experimental rigor and reproducibility concerns. Overall, the evidence largely supports the conclusions despite these limited methodological gaps. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are supported by experiments and evaluations: artist-specific LSTM models are compared against a comprehensive n-gram baseline, manual annotations (line-level fluency/coherence and style-matching) and automated metrics (rhyme density, max similarity regression) are reported, and the prior semi-automatic repetition-penalty method is fully automated. Mathematical derivations are not central and none are highlighted as problematic; experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and multiple annotation metrics. Minor technical concerns remain (heuristic preprocessing may leave non-verse text in the corpus, and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release), but these do not materially undermine the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The summary indicates the paper's main claims are empirically supported: artist-specific LSTM models were compared to a strong n-gram (1–9) baseline using complementary manual annotations (line-level fluency/coherence, style-matching) and automated metrics (rhyme density, max similarity regression), and known limitations are acknowledged (vocabulary integration, preprocessing noise). There are no mathematical proofs to evaluate and hyperparameters are reported, but the summary does not mention statistical tests or code release, and the dataset preprocessing may leave non-verse text—issues that are minor but leave some experimental rigor and reproducibility concerns. Overall, the evidence largely supports the conclusions despite these limited methodological gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by experiments and evaluations: artist-specific LSTM models are compared against a comprehensive n-gram baseline, manual annotations (line-level fluency/coherence and style-matching) and automated metrics (rhyme density, max similarity regression) are reported, and the prior semi-automatic repetition-penalty method is fully automated. Mathematical derivations are not central and none are highlighted as problematic; experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and multiple annotation metrics. Minor technical concerns remain (heuristic preprocessing may leave non-verse text in the corpus, and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release), but these do not materially undermine the main experimental conclusions.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP topic—creative language generation and its evaluation—by proposing a comprehensive manual evaluation methodology, automating a prior semi-automatic repetition penalty, and providing a manually annotated corpus for 13 rap artists, all of which are relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. It uses community-expected methods and evaluation standards (LSTM vs. n-gram baselines, rhyme density, similarity metrics, manual fluency/coherence and style-matching annotations) appropriate for such venues. Minor scope concerns are that the work is narrowly focused on rap lyrics and has acknowledged dataset/preprocessing and model limitations (e.g., vocabulary integration), which makes it a very good but not perfectly general fit for the broadest top-tier venues. | B(5): The paper addresses a core NLP task (creative language generation / rap lyric ghostwriting) and contributes a comprehensive manual evaluation methodology, an automated improvement to prior evaluation (repetition penalty), and a new annotated corpus for 13 artists—materials and issues of clear interest to the NLP community. It uses standard modeling (artist-specific LSTM vs n-gram baselines) and evaluation metrics (rhyme density, similarity, Match%) that align with venue expectations for empirical and evaluation-focused work. The focus on evaluation methodology and dataset release makes it well positioned for a top-tier NLP/ML conference audience interested in generation and evaluation research.

It uses community-expected methods and evaluation standards (LSTM vs. n-gram baselines, rhyme density, similarity metrics, manual fluency/coherence and style-matching annotations) appropriate for such venues. Minor scope concerns are that the work is narrowly focused on rap lyrics and has acknowledged dataset/preprocessing and model limitations (e.g., vocabulary integration), which makes it a very good but not perfectly general fit for the broadest top-tier venues.

It uses standard modeling (artist-specific LSTM vs n-gram baselines) and evaluation metrics (rhyme density, similarity, Match%) that align with venue expectations for empirical and evaluation-focused work. The focus on evaluation methodology and dataset release makes it well positioned for a top-tier NLP/ML conference audience interested in generation and evaluation research.

main_claims: A comprehensive manual evaluation methodology for generated rap verses along fluency, coherence, and style matching is presented.
An improvement and full automation of a previously semi-automatic evaluation method (Potash et al., 2015) that automatically penalizes repetitive text is introduced.
A corpus of authentic lyrics for 13 rap artists, manually annotated for stylistic similarity, is created and made publicly available.
An LSTM-based ghostwriter model is evaluated and shown to generalize better than an n-gram baseline, while highlighting limitations such as the LSTM's inability to integrate new vocabulary.
_summary.methodology: Train artist-specific LSTM RNN models to generate rap verses and evaluate them with complementary manual annotations (line-level fluency and coherence, style-matching) and automated metrics (rhyme density and maximum similarity regression), comparing to an n-gram baseline and fully automating a prior semi-automatic procedure with a repetition penalty.
datasets: The Original Hip-Hop (Rap) Lyrics Archive - OHHLA.com corpus of lyrics for 13 rap artists (authentic verse, manually annotated for style matching)
Artists explicitly mentioned in the corpus: Eminem, Jay Z, Tupac, Aesop Rock, GZA, Sage Francis, DMX, Drake, Fabolous, Notorious B.I.G., Lil' Wayne
metrics: rhyme density (number of rhymed syllables / total syllables)
MatchA% (joint annotator correctness when they agree)
Raw agreement % (annotator agreement on chosen verse)
manual line-level fluency and coherence ratings
limitations: Automated evaluation cannot assess certain aspects such as vocabulary, tone, and themes of a target artist.
The LSTM model has difficulty integrating new vocabulary, limiting desirable similarity scores.
Preprocessing was heuristic and the dataset may still contain non-verse text (e.g., dialogue or chorus lines).
Negative iteration values occur because early/generated outputs can exceed the artist's average rhyme density.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - creative language generation (rap lyric ghostwriting)
